speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interviewing Anita Karl. The date is December 10, 1995. This is the beginning of tape four. And you were telling me that you ran back to that little hut where the border guards were. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then two hours later, my mother said one of the other girls needed to go to the bathroom. So the Russians were already used to us going and coming, and they didn't pay much attention. But this time my mother took me by the arm, not my other sister, and she said, you better run when I tell you to run. And that was more of a command and overtook my fear. And I run, and we crossed the border into Berlin, into the American zone. And there, we went looking for the people that had told us that were taking care of the refugees. And they had the UNRA-- the United Nations Relief, the joint organization. They had several. And we came in in the bitter winter cold, my mother and the three of us. And the friend of my mother went to look for another person she knew in Berlin that had survived the war. And she wanted to get in touch with him, because he was a wealthy man and he would take care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my mother went, in the meantime, to seek a little bit of help for the first days. So the man who interviewed her asked her her name, who she was. And she says that she's Malca Karl Brandt from Lemberg, and these are her little children. So he started laughing. He says, who do you want to tell this story? There are no survivors from Lemberg, and let alone a woman with three children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the people that survived, they gave the children away. They gave them to neighbors. They gave them to churches, to convents. Nobody survived with three little children. That is impossible. And from the ghetto from Lemberg there are no survivors. You are telling me a story. Get away. Go away. And he threw us out. Instead of giving us some milk, like the Russians, or something to eat, he simply threw us out on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Luckily, at the same moment came my mother's friend with this man. And my mother tells him, what, they're throwing me out. They don't believe me. So the man-- a Mr. Bank was his last name-- he says to this other interviewer, how dare you? Do you know what kind of family these people are, what [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], what Jewish people, Orthodox, what family-- well-known, not only in Lemberg, but in many other places? They were the aristocrats-- yeah, they were-- of Judaism, and you dare to throw them out? Come, he says. Come, Mrs. Karl, with me. I will take you to my house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took us to an apartment. And he gave us a place to sleep and told us about his story and what he was doing. But then my mother and him went-- she didn't want to stay there, because he was smuggling things from one side of Berlin to the other. He was a bachelor, alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother wanted to be with the Americans. She wanted to be taken care of through the proper channels. And she wanted to be in touch with the survivors, just in case somebody else survived. So they you put this into the displaced persons camp in Berlin. And this was 19-- the beginning of 1994, and the people received us-- I don't think it was 1994.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, 1995. The beginning of 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: The beginning-- yes, the beginning of 1945 in Berlin. And the American people took wonderful care of us. Immediately, they took us in. They gave us rations. We were four, so they gave us four times everything. They gave us four cartons of cigarettes and four kilos coffee, four kilos of sugar. Everything was four, because we were four. And so that allowed my mother to trade for other things, you know, that we needed. And they gave us lodging. We were in buildings, special buildings, for us. And they started to vaccinate everybody, and I was so scared of those needles. So my mother took all the vaccinations instead of me. They had to give them to somebody, they said, so my mother took them. I wouldn't take them. And they requested my mother to please take care of the orphans, of the babies, so she had under her care-- she wanted to work. She wanted to do something. She had under her care 50 infants, orphans from the war. Then later, people from Palestine came and took them to Palestine. But for a year we were there in Berlin, and then we were taken to Stuttgart, which was another displaced person camp. And my mother decided she wanted to learn a trade. She wanted to do something with her free hours, so she started learning how to do hats. And she took classes in that, and she would tell us about the stories of her family. And she was living with-- with the pain, but remembering each and every one of them to us and telling us the stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me for interrupting. Was this at the time that the story happened with the Christmas tree that you told me about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Berlin still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us that story? I think it's important.

speaker: interviewee
text: When my mother told us that we are no longer Jews, I fought that for a long time. But when we got to Berlin, it was Christmastime, and everybody had a Christmas tree. And I wanted my Christmas tree very badly, and I said to my mother, Mom, please. I know we are Jews. I understand everything. I'm trying to, I said. But I want a Christmas tree, because that is my tradition. I want my--

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother said, how am I going into a displaced person's houses? Everybody's Jewish survivors of the Holocaust. How do you expect me to bring in a Christmas tree? I said, I don't care. You-- any way you can, you would be able to bring me my Christmas tree. And my mother brought me my Christmas tree.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that night she showed me a Jewish prayer to say instead of kneeling and saying the pater nostrum. She says you don't have to do like this anymore. You can sit on the-- on the bed and you can say, [SPEAKING HEBREW]. This means keep me, my God, from all harm. And then you go to sleep. So I sat on the bed and I said [SPEAKING HEBREW], with her repeating, as I make my grandson repeat today. [SPEAKING HEBREW] But when she left the room, and the lights were out, I went on my knees, and I did the Father, who art in heaven, just in case. I wanted to be with both on the good side. And I did it for a long time, for months, until slowly and gently she brought me into the path of our ancestors. And while we were then in Stuttgart, and I still was very much afraid of people in uniform, there were soldiers-- American, German-- or people wearing a uniform for any reason. It was frightening. It was so frightening that I couldn't be in the same room with somebody who wore a uniform or be close or near. I-- I had-- the panic was with me all day long. We were scarred. We were scarred for the rest of our lives. Until today, anything that has to do which soldiers is difficult for me to bear. In Stuttgart, the friend of my mother went to Miami, where she had her family. And we had no place to go. Nobody was calling for us. Nobody had survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided to go to Palestine. The Jews were going with the children on the exodus, or other means. Illegal though they were, we had a chance to get to. The only inconvenience, the only terrible thing, was to be, again, in Cyprus at a concentration camp that was ruled by the English.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, the friend of my mother got to Miami, and she put an ad in the paper. We knew that our uncle, the Dr. Karl who had been expelled from Vienna, from Austria, had gone to Peru. But we had no way of knowing where or what the address was. So my mother's friend put an ad in a German-speaking newspaper-- Jewish German-speaking newspaper-- called the which circulated in the North and South America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in New York, a patient of my-- my uncle's read it and forwarded it to him to see if this was his family. And sure enough, he wrote to us and asked my mother, are you the Malca Karl married to my brother, Samuel? And are these your children? And please, tell me where are my parents and my brothers? I haven't heard from them for five years. So my mother had the sad task to write him a letter and tell him that they had all been killed, exterminated, and that from his family no one survived, and we were the only ones that were left. So he said, please-- he wrote back a letter-- I am sending you first-class tickets, food packages, whatever you need. Don't go to Palestine. Come to Lima, to Peru, so we can be united and be a family again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he brought us over. We left Stuttgart. My mother agreed, and he sent us lots and lots of things for us. Some arrived. Some didn't. And we had booked passage. He had booked passage-- passage for us from LeHavre-- from LeHavre to Rio, to Brazil, on one of the best ships afloat at that time, passenger-wise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we waited in Paris six months. And again people came from Palestine to take the children. And this woman said to me, do you want to come? Do you want to come? And no, we-- we were going to go with mother to-- to-- to Lima to be with our uncle. And I was sure that all the streets were paved in gold and that we would have a wonderful life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we arrived at LeHavre-- ship, and they tell us, this is your ship. And we see a freighter-- a cargo ship. And my mother said, but this is not my reservation. Well, you want to go, this is it. They had us on a cattle ship that had brought cattle from Argentina, an Argentinian cattle ship. And that needed freight to go back, and we were the freight. There were, like, 180 people on this ship, little ship. Took us, I don't know, two weeks. The crossing was horrible. And we arrived in Brazil, and in Rio, all the Jewish people were waiting for us. It was Shavuot. It was a holiday. And the Jewish people from Brazil were waiting to take us all. They were fighting over who is going to take a family with them for the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took us over. This lady, Paulina-- Madame Paulina-- she took us over, and she had a feast prepared. And she says, I am not eating anything. The children are not eating. So she says to my mother, what? Do they want something else? There were bananas, oranges-- food that we had never seen before.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was white rolls, croissants, white bread that we had never seen before. And I wanted a piece of bread, a piece of stale, black bread. That was my desire, so she went and brought me a piece of rye bread, so I could be happy. And then we flew to Lima, and the streets were not paved in gold. That was a disappointment, but my uncle, who was waiting for us, was a wonderful human being. He took us in. He gave us love. He was a bachelor. He had never married. And he fell in love with my mother, and although the Bible says that you have to marry your brother-in-law's wife, he did it out of love. He was a compassionate man, a very, very wonderful husband to her. They had 25 blissful years together. He adopted us legally and raised us. And he was so unselfish that he didn't expect us to call him Papa. He said, I want you to remember your father, your papa, and you can call us-- call me uncle, and that is fine. And then when we got married and had children, he became the Opi, the Opapa. And since, is gone, is no longer with us, and my mother is gone too. And our life in Lima was beautiful. We had everything. We had a wonderful education that he gave us. We had our little family together. But the sadness of growing up without cousins, grandparents, and uncles was devastating to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I always will never forget, as long as I live, the atrocities that were committed. I will never forget. What gave these people the right to take away what was mine, to take away my family, to take away my father and my cousins and grandparents and to grow up without their love?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, in her later years, wrote a testimony of these things. And she has done it for us, for her children, so we would not forget. The book is called because she did escape, that day, from the ghetto to life. She did not wait for death to come to her and her family. And through the pages of this fantastic and marvelous book, you see her courage, her strength, and her will to live and as a mother, the protection to our children. The book has been a big success. It has been a best-seller for many, many months, and until today there are still people looking for it, wanting to buy it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the book there that you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the book I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you share something with us out of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And it is in Spanish, so I will try to translate it, just a few pages here and there. And especially her-- the compliments she got. She dedicated this to us. And she says, 'I will try to relate for my children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren, and all the generations of the future, the tragic events that were done by the Fuhrer of the Nazis and which I was able to survive with my little three daughters. I want through this story make it perennial and remember all the names of those saints sacrificed to the German [? raft ?] that shared with me a quality of family and friends that no longer exists, those terrible years in Poland during the Second World War.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Between the millions-- the six millions-- of Jews that were killed during the war, remember that a million and a half were children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the statement that you would like to make to the future generations that are going to see this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first statement, above all, is try to find this book and read this book. It should be in every family today alike, Gentiles or Jews, because it is the courage and the will to survive, and that no terror and no horrors are stronger. We are stronger. We are the people. And to the generations to come, look into the past, because it is the only way that you can prevent these massacres in the future. Do not forget us. Remember us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much. Who is this, please? These are my grandparents, Ida and Mark Karl, and two of his-- of their children. They are in a resort place in the mountains. What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Samuel Karl. And it is in Poland, in Lvov. And he was probably 29 or 28 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Malca Karl, in Poland. And she-- this picture must've been taken in '39-- no, earlier, earlier than '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these people, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one is my sister, younger sister, Esther Karl.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then is my mother, Malca, and Joel Karl, my uncle, and then Father. Then it's me, Anita Karl, and Felice Karl, my oldest sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was a stake in Lima, Peru, in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my second father, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-E and my mother, Mali Karl in Lima, Peru also, in 1960s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my children, my daughter-in-law, Sharon, and my son, Samuel. It is their wedding picture, taken in Miami in 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are also my children, my son-in-law, Jerry Brodsky, and my daughter, Michelle Brodsky Karl, on their wedding day here in Miami in 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these beautiful children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first two are my daughter's sons, Andrew and Joel. And the third one is my son's son, Steven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please explain this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a family picture as we are today with my two sisters, Felice and Esther, and our children and one grandchild. The picture was taken at a wedding in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell us what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the scarf that I have kept all along with me and I never go anywhere without it. It is the last time he wrapped his arms around me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these beautiful people with you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: These people are my children and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you name them for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson, Steven, and my grandson, Andrew, and my grandson, Joel. This is my daughter, Michelle, and my nephew, David-- he is the son of my sister, Felice-- my son, Sammy, my son-in-law, Jerry, and my daughter-in-law, Sharon. This is my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michelle, would you like to say something about your mother today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to say that I'm very proud that she has contributed in writing the book. And she's an exceptional woman, and we are all very proud of her for all that she's done.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you like to say something about your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: DAVID (NEPHEW): I feel exactly the same way. She's an exceptional person, and that's what I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does anyone else to share about Anita? I will. She's a wonderful woman. Thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. SHARON (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Thank you.
